raw_case:
Case report of central serous chorioretinopathy with intraretinal fluid and normal fundus fluorescein and indocyanine green angiography


Abstract

A 73-year-old man was diagnosed with central serous chorioretinopathy (CSCR). He had atypical features including a normal indocyanine green angiography (ICG) and fundus fluorescein angiography (FFA), uncommon age group for initial diagnosis and a finding of intraretinal fluid. This case report is the first of our knowledge that exemplifies this type of unusual clinical presentation for CSCR.

Introduction

Central serous chorioretinopathy (CSCR) is the fourth most common retinal disease with 9.9 of 100 000 men and 1.7 of 100 000 women affected [1]. Despite its high incidence, the pathophysiology isn’t well understood. A pathophysiology proposed is that the hyperpermeability of choroid capillaries results in retinal pigment epithelial (RPE) dysfunction [2]. CSCR is correlated with prior steroid use, Helicobacter pylori infection, Type A personality, pregnancy, and hyperopia [3].The most common presenting symptoms are central scotoma, reduced contrast sensitivity, micropsia, reduced colour saturation, refraction shift, metamorphopsia [1].The natural course of acute CSCR is self-limiting within 2–3 months [1]. Chronic CSCR is defined as persistent subretinal fluid for more than 6 months [4].We present an atypical case of unilateral chronic CSCR. To our knowledge, there is no previous literature demonstrating this.

Case report

A 73-year-old male self-presented to the eye emergency with 2 weeks of left blurred vision and loss of contrast. The onset of symptoms were a few days following chemotherapy for diffuse, large B cell lymphoma. He had a known history of poor vision in his right eye since childhood. Otherwise, his background included hypertension.On examination, his corrected visual acuity on Snellen chart was 6/9.6-2 for his left eye and 6/60 for his right eye (baseline). Anterior segment examination was unremarkable. On dilated fundus exam, his right eye had longstanding macular atrophy. His left eye had a 0.3 pink disc, no swelling, with a blunted macular reflex. This can be seen in Fig. 1.Optical coherence tomography (OCT) demonstrated an atrophied right macular. Whilst the left eye showed a thickened choroid, multiple pigment epithelial detachments (PED), subretinal fluid and intraretinal fluid nasally. This is shown in Figs 2 and 3.The patient had normal macular FFA and ICG as seen in Fig. 4.The patient was diagnosed with CSCR.In the first instance, it was presumed that the CSCR was secondary to prednisone from his chemotherapy regime. He had no family history of CSCR, no symptoms of H. pylori infection and no underlying refractory error. In discussion with his haematologist, the steroid therapy was weaned and ceased.Despite 9 months since cessation of steroid therapy, the patient had persistent left subretinal and intraretinal fluid and fluctuating corrected visual acuity ranging from 6/6-2 to 6/9. His ophthalmologist opted against invasive treatment as it was the patient’s better seeing eye. This was now called chronic CSCR, and the decision was made to monitor.Finally, the patient was seen 12 months later. His left eye vision was 6/9 and his OCT showed a serous PED, with resolution of subretinal or intraretinal fluid as seen in Fig. 5.

Discussion

The diagnosis of CSCR was made by the patient’s ophthalmologist, meeting diagnostic criteria as seen in Table 1. The patient meets major criteria with an OCT showing serous retinal detachment, and RPE alterations seen in Figs 2 and 3, respectively. Figure 2 displays a subfoveal choroidal thickness of 454 μm meeting the minor criteria.Importantly, this patient had clinical features inconsistent with intraocular lymphoma with no anterior or posterior segment inflammation, no optic nerve swelling and no retinal or subretinal lesions across multiple visits in 2 years [6]. Intracerebral disease was excluded on magnetic resonance brain imaging. Hypergammaglobinaemia disorders were considered due to the normal FFA and ICG and the history of B cell lymphoma. However, the patient had normal serum immunoglobulins. Additionally, a bone marrow aspirate demonstrated normal plasma cells and morphology.There are a few aspects of his case that makes it an atypical chronic CSCR.Firstly, he was outside of the mean age of incidence for CSCR which was found to be 41 and 39 years of age in two cohort studies, respectively [7, 8]. This exemplifies the importance of still considering CSCR in older age groups.Secondly, CSCR usually has supporting features for the diagnosis on ICG and FFA which are not demonstrated in this case. The most common signs on FFA at the macular are a pinpoint leak in the early phase, at the mid-phase, the hyper-fluorescence increases in intensity and in the late-phase evolves into a ‘smokestack’ or ‘ink-blot’ pattern [9]. ICG, in CSCR, displays choroidal filing delay in the early phase, hyperpermeability of choroidal vessels with multi-focal hyper-fluorescence around the macular in the mid-phase and fading of this hyper-fluorescence in the late phase [9, 10]. As demonstrated in Fig. 4, the patient had a normal FFA and ICG in mid-phase.Finally, the patient had intraretinal fluid. A study found that hyper-reflectivity in the outer nuclear layers was described in over 90% of CSCR patients and this correlated with subretinal fluid. Intraretinal fluid wasn’t seen [11]. This demonstrates the importance of still considering CSCR in patients with intraretinal fluid.Treatment for CSCR doesn’t have a consensus amongst clinicians. Management includes laser photocoagulation, intravitreal anti-vascular endothelial growth factor injections, and mineralocorticoid or glucocorticoid receptor antagonist [12]. Treatment options are limited for this individual. Firstly, steroid receptor antagonist therapy cannot be implemented given the patient’s co-existing medical condition of diffuse large B cell lymphoma. Additionally, laser therapy or intravitreal injections were considered; however, the treating ophthalmologist believed it was high risk due to the contralateral eye having longstanding poor vision.Although the patient’s subretinal and intraretinal fluid has resolved; It’s possible that he will have a recurrence of CSCR as a previous study found that 31% of patients had recurrence [7].

Conclusion

The key points from this case are to consider CSCR as a differential in older patients, those with a normal ICG or FFA and those with intraretinal fluid. It’s important to consider CSCR for patients presenting similarly as it can be self-resolving and not require invasive treatment.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 73-year-old male
- **Chief Complaint:** 2 weeks of left blurred vision and loss of contrast
- **History of Present Illness:** Symptoms began a few days after chemotherapy for diffuse large B-cell lymphoma. Known history of poor right eye vision since childhood.
- **Past Medical History:** Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, hypertension, longstanding right macular atrophy
- **Personal History:** None provided
- **Family History:** No family history of CSCR
- **Physical Examination:** 
  - Left eye: Corrected visual acuity 6/9.6-2, pink optic disc (0.3), blunted macular reflex
  - Right eye: Corrected visual acuity 6/60, longstanding macular atrophy
- **Ancillary Tests:**
  - OCT (Optical Coherence Tomography): Left eye showed thickened choroid, multiple pigment epithelial detachments, subretinal fluid, and nasal intraretinal fluid. Right eye showed macular atrophy.
  - Fundus fluorescein angiography (FFA) and indocyanine green angiography (ICG): Normal findings
  - MRI of the brain: Excluded intracerebral disease
  - Bone marrow aspirate: Normal plasma cell morphology; no hypergammaglobulinemia disorders
- **Other Relevant Information:** Steroid therapy suspected as a contributing factor, which was subsequently weaned off. Chronic CSCR persisted despite steroid cessation but eventually showed resolution of fluid on a 12-month follow-up.

---
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Considerations:**
   - The presenting symptoms and OCT findings initially pointed to central serous chorioretinopathy (CSCR). Despite the atypical findings (normal FFA/ICG and old age), it was included in the differential diagnosis because of the presence of subretinal fluid and pigment epithelial detachments.

2. **Exclusions Based on Clinical Features:**
   - **Intraocular lymphoma:** Excluded due to lack of anterior/posterior segment inflammation, optic nerve swelling, or retinal/subretinal lesions across two years, along with normal MRI findings.
   - **Hypergammaglobulinemia disorders:** Excluded based on normal serum immunoglobulin levels and bone marrow aspirate.
   - **Other retinal conditions (e.g., age-related macular degeneration):** Excluded based on the unilateral presentation, absence of drusen, and findings inconsistent with other retinal pathologies.

3. **Final Diagnosis Selection:**
   - CSCR was diagnosed based on OCT evidence of serous retinal detachment and RPE changes, as well as supportive findings such as thickened choroid. The absence of FFA/ICG abnormalities, intraretinal fluid, and the patient’s age made this an unusual case. However, the self-limiting course and absence of alternative conditions supported the diagnosis.

---
generate_case.final_diagnosis:
Chronic central serous chorioretinopathy (CSCR) with atypical features, including older age, normal FFA/ICG, and intraretinal fluid.

---

results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> **Analyze the Chief Complaint and History of Present Illness:** The patient presents with a recent onset of left blurred vision and loss of contrast. This suggests a macular or optic nerve issue in the left eye. The onset shortly after chemotherapy for lymphoma is a crucial temporal relationship that needs to be considered. Chemotherapy can have ocular side effects, and lymphoma itself can sometimes have ocular manifestations, although less likely in this acute presentation given the test results.

<step 2> **Evaluate Past Medical History and Risk Factors:** The patient has diffuse large B-cell lymphoma and is undergoing chemotherapy.  Steroid therapy is suspected as a contributing factor, which is a well-known risk factor for Central Serous Chorioretinopathy (CSCR). Hypertension is present but less directly relevant to the acute onset of visual symptoms. The longstanding right macular atrophy is an important baseline for the right eye, indicating a pre-existing chronic condition in the other eye, but seemingly unrelated to the acute left eye symptoms.

<step 3> **Interpret Physical Examination Findings:**  The left eye examination shows reduced visual acuity (6/9.6-2), a normal optic disc, and a blunted macular reflex. Reduced acuity and blunted macular reflex point towards macular pathology. The normal optic disc makes optic neuritis less likely as the primary diagnosis. The right eye findings are consistent with the known macular atrophy.

<step 4> **Analyze Ancillary Test Results - OCT:** The OCT of the left eye is highly significant.  The findings of thickened choroid, multiple pigment epithelial detachments (PEDs), subretinal fluid (SRF), and nasal intraretinal fluid (IRF) are collectively strongly suggestive of Central Serous Chorioretinopathy (CSCR). These findings indicate fluid accumulation in and under the retina, characteristic of CSCR. The right eye OCT confirms macular atrophy.

<step 5> **Interpret Ancillary Test Results - Angiography (FFA & ICG):** The normal FFA and ICG are somewhat atypical for classic CSCR, which often shows leakage on FFA and choroidal hyperpermeability on ICG. However, atypical presentations of CSCR, especially in the early stages or in steroid-induced cases, can sometimes have minimal or no leakage on initial angiography.  It's important to note that normal angiography doesn't completely rule out CSCR, particularly when other clinical and OCT findings are strongly suggestive.

<step 6> **Evaluate Ancillary Test Results - MRI and Bone Marrow Aspirate:** The normal brain MRI excludes intracranial pathology, such as optic neuritis or compressive lesions affecting the visual pathway. The normal bone marrow aspirate rules out hyperviscosity syndromes or plasma cell dyscrasias that could cause similar retinal findings.

<step 7> **Consider the Impact of Steroid Therapy and Clinical Course:** The suspicion of steroid therapy as a contributing factor and its subsequent weaning is a crucial piece of information. Steroids are a well-established cause of CSCR. The persistence of chronic CSCR despite steroid cessation but eventual resolution over 12 months strongly supports a diagnosis of CSCR, even in the context of initially normal angiography. The spontaneous resolution without specific treatment (other than steroid cessation) is also typical for the natural course of CSCR.

<step 8> **Synthesize all Findings to Formulate a Diagnosis:**  Considering the patient's age, symptoms, OCT findings highly suggestive of CSCR, the history of chemotherapy and suspected steroid use (a known CSCR risk factor), and the clinical course of persistence and eventual resolution, the most probable diagnosis is Central Serous Chorioretinopathy (CSCR), likely steroid-induced, with an atypical initial angiographic presentation.

### Answer:
Central Serous Chorioretinopathy (CSCR), likely steroid-induced
